name: Gradient Boosting Classifier
summary: Scikit-learn's GradientBoostingClassifier builds trees sequentially using first-order gradients to correct the errors of previous estimators, following the classical gradient boosting machine framework. It uses level-wise tree growth with exact split finding and full sorting at each node, providing a stable baseline without external dependencies.
use_when: Small to medium tabular datasets requiring a reliable baseline without installing external dependencies. Environments with strict security or compliance requirements limiting package installations. Educational contexts teaching fundamental gradient boosting mechanics from first principles.
avoid_when: Large datasets with millions of rows where XGBoost or LightGBM train orders of magnitude faster. GPU-accelerated training requirements. Production systems needing built-in early stopping, distributed training, or native categorical feature support.
pros: Native scikit-learn implementation requires no extra dependencies beyond the standard sklearn package
Solid baseline accuracy for structured tabular classification and regression without library complexity
Supports sample_weight for handling class imbalance, custom loss weighting, and cost-sensitive learning
Stable API with consistent behavior across scikit-learn versions, making it reliable for long-term maintenance
cons: Slower than XGBoost and LightGBM on large datasets due to lack of histogram optimization and level-wise growth
No native GPU acceleration for training or inference, limiting scalability on modern hardware
No early stopping by default until scikit-learn 0.20; earlier versions require manual validation loop implementation
Level-wise tree growth with full sorting at each split is memory-intensive on high-dimensional datasets

key_hyperparams.name: n_estimators
key_hyperparams.note: Number of boosting stages. Each stage fits a tree to the negative gradient. Increase to 200-500 but monitor for overfitting since early stopping is not automatic in older sklearn versions.

key_hyperparams.name: max_depth
key_hyperparams.note: Maximum depth of individual trees. Default 3 is intentionally shallow; increase to 5-8 for more complex relationships but watch for overfitting since there is no built-in regularization beyond tree depth.

key_hyperparams.name: learning_rate
key_hyperparams.note: Shrinkage factor applied to each tree's contribution. Lower to 0.01-0.05 and increase n_estimators for better regularization and smoother convergence, trading training time for generalization.

key_hyperparams.name: subsample
key_hyperparams.note: Fraction of samples used for fitting each tree. Set to 0.5-0.8 to implement stochastic gradient boosting and reduce variance through random row sampling.

key_hyperparams.name: min_samples_leaf
key_hyperparams.note: Minimum samples required in leaf nodes. Increase to 2-20 to smooth predictions and reduce overfitting on small datasets, especially when combined with a shallow max_depth.

quick_start:
from sklearn.ensemble import GradientBoostingClassifier

clf = GradientBoostingClassifier(
    n_estimators=200,
    max_depth=3,
    learning_rate=0.1,
    subsample=0.8,
    min_samples_leaf=2,
    random_state=42
)
clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
preds = clf.predict(X_test)
